policy_statement: Increase the pace of local floodplain assessments to identify flood hazards (DEC).
mechanism_description: Increasing the pace of local floodplain assessments improves identification of flood hazards and supports better flood-risk planning and preparedness.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → improved risk information & planning → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ damages/disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Local governments/agencies accelerate and expand floodplain mapping/assessment activities (data collection, modeling, updating flood maps). (2) Mediating process: Better hazard characterization (extent, depth, frequency, future risk) improves risk communication and enables risk-based decisions (zoning, siting, design standards, evacuation planning, prioritization of protection projects). (3) Outcome: Reduced flood risk and impacts—lower asset and life-safety exposure, improved preparedness and response, and increased resilience to extreme precipitation/sea-level-rise-driven flooding.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood impacts (exposure/vulnerability) through better identification of flood hazards and improved preparedness/planning.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Flood hazard information → planning/preparedness actions → reduced exposure/vulnerability to flooding (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While better information can indirectly influence emissions (e.g., avoiding rebuilds) or efficiency, the direct and largest intended climate effect is managing climate-driven flood risk and reducing impacts.  
3) Edge cases applied: If assessments are used to justify nature-based flood storage or specific infrastructure retrofits, those would be secondary implementation choices; the assessment mechanism itself remains risk identification/planning (adaptation).  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy accelerating floodplain assessments is fundamentally about hazard identification and adaptation planning, regardless of location.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Secondary subtype (e.g., governance/information, land-use planning, emergency management) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge case: If a policy frames floodplain assessments primarily as a prerequisite for mitigation-linked land-use changes (e.g., steering growth to reduce transport emissions), that would be atypical; the dominant effect of floodplain assessment acceleration remains adaptation via flood-risk reduction.
key_indicators: "Increase pace of floodplain assessments"; "identify flood hazards"
co_benefits: Improved public safety/emergency management; better land-use decision-making and insurance/risk communication (general disaster-risk reduction beyond climate-specific framing)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be construed as an infrastructure-investment if it implied major funded acquisition (e.g., LiDAR, modeling contracts), but the text only specifies speeding up assessments, which is primarily a planning/risk-information action.
